items.question_text: A pilot files a VFR flight plan from Miami to San Juan through the Miami Oceanic CTA at FL 190. Is this operation permitted?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flight plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs are permitted only below FL 180. At FL 190 the aircraft is at or above FL 180 and therefore outside the permitted altitude range.

choices.text: No, VFR flight plans in those CTAs are permitted only below FL 180.
Yes, VFR flight plans are permitted in all Oceanic CTAs at any altitude.
Yes, but only if weather conditions meet 14 CFR 91.155 VFR minimums.
No, VFR flight plans are never permitted in any Oceanic CTA.

items.question_text: A pilot operating under a VFR flight plan in the Oakland FIR asks to continue 120 NM seaward from the shoreline. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flight plan operations within the Oakland FIR are permitted only when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace. At 120 NM, the aircraft would be beyond the permitted distance.

choices.text: Yes, as long as the aircraft remains in controlled airspace.
No, Oakland FIR VFR flight plan operations are limited to less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline.
Yes, but only during daylight hours.
No, VFR flight plans are prohibited in the entire Oakland FIR.

items.question_text: A letter of agreement between two oceanic facilities specifies that action to obtain a missing position report must be taken no later than 8 minutes after the control estimate. The default Chapter 8 deadline is 10 minutes. Which deadline applies?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, the 10-minute deadline applies "unless otherwise specified." When an LOA or directive specifies a different deadline, that specified deadline supersedes the default. The controller follows the LOA-specified 8-minute deadline for that operation.

choices.text: The 10-minute Chapter 8 deadline, because LOAs cannot change published minima.
The controller may choose either deadline based on workload.
The 8-minute LOA deadline, because the paragraph permits a different deadline when otherwise specified.
Both deadlines apply; the controller must attempt to obtain the report by the earlier time.

items.question_text: Two aircraft are entering oceanic airspace on courses that diverge by 20 degrees. Radar shows the aircraft are horizontally separated at the edge of known radar coverage, but the separation between them is decreasing. Does separation exist under the course divergence rule?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-2, course divergence separation requires BOTH: (a) courses diverge by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established, AND (b) the aircraft are horizontally radar separated and separation is increasing at the edge of known radar coverage. Decreasing separation fails condition (b).

choices.text: Yes, because the 20-degree divergence exceeds the 15-degree minimum.
No, because both conditions must be met and the separation is decreasing, not increasing.
Yes, because radar separation exists at the edge of coverage, regardless of trend.
No, because course divergence requires at least 30 degrees.

items.question_text: An aircraft is transitioning from oceanic airspace into an area of known radar coverage. Flight data indicates standard transponder equipment, but the controller knows the transponder is inoperative. What does the radar identification transition procedure require regarding transponder status?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-5d, flight data must indicate standard transponder equipment AND there must be no known information that the transponder is not operating. The procedure is disqualified when the transponder is known to be inoperative, even if the flight data indicates the equipment is installed.

choices.text: The procedure may be applied as long as flight data indicates the equipment, regardless of known status.
The procedure may be applied if the radar-identified aircraft can provide visual separation.
The procedure requires both flight data indicating a standard transponder and no known information that it is inoperative; known inoperative status disqualifies it.
The procedure does not require a transponder in oceanic transition airspace.

items.question_text: In addition to oceanic airspace transitions, to which specific control areas does the radar identification transition procedure also apply?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-5g, this procedure is also applicable to aircraft in transit from oceanic airspace into Guam Control Area (CTA), San Juan CTA, and Honolulu CTA radar coverage areas.

choices.text: Anchorage CTA, Oakland CTA, and New York CTA only.
Miami CTA, Houston CTA, and Arctic CTA only.
All CTAs; no specific limitation applies.
Guam CTA, San Juan CTA, and Honolulu CTA.

items.question_text: A controller needs to separate an aircraft from a temporary moving airspace reservation. How does the longitudinal separation method differ from the lateral method?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-3, longitudinal separation clears the aircraft so that the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation. Lateral separation ensures the route protected airspace does not overlap the moving reservation's time-dependent geographical area. Longitudinal is relative to aircraft in the reservation; lateral is relative to the reservation boundary.

choices.text: Longitudinal separation applies a minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft in the reservation; lateral separation prevents protected airspace overlap with the reservation area.
Both methods use the same technique; the only difference is the direction of measurement.
Longitudinal separation uses a buffer of one-half the lateral minimum; lateral separation uses the full minimum.
Longitudinal separation applies only to stationary reservations; lateral applies only to moving ones.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies between supersonic aircraft of different types operating at different Mach numbers?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3a2, 15 minutes applies between aircraft in supersonic flight that do not qualify for the 10-minute provision. The 10-minute minimum requires both aircraft in level flight at the same Mach number or same type in cruise climb.

choices.text: 10 minutes.
15 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to nonturbojet aircraft operating outside the West Atlantic Route System (WATRS)?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3c2, 30 minutes applies between nonturbojet aircraft operating outside the WATRS. The 20-minute minimum under 8-7-3c1 applies only within WATRS.

choices.text: 15 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.
10 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what conditions must be satisfied before an aircraft on a reciprocal track may be cleared to climb or descend through the altitude of another aircraft using ADS-C?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3e2, an ADS-C position report on at least one aircraft must have been received beyond the passing point, and the aircraft must have passed each other by the applicable separation minimum.

choices.text: Both aircraft must report passing the same waypoint simultaneously.
The pilot of either aircraft requests the climb or descent.
Only after 15 minutes of vertical separation has been maintained.
An ADS-C position report on at least one aircraft has been received beyond the passing point and they have passed by the applicable minimum.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what lateral separation minimum applies between aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval that are operating on routes within WATRS?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-4d, 90 NM or 1.5 degrees latitude applies between aircraft not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10 that operate on routes or in areas within WATRS, the San Juan CTA/FIR, or the Atlantic portion of the Miami CTA/FIR.

choices.text: 90 NM or 1.5 degrees latitude.
50 NM.
60 NM or 1 degree latitude.
120 NM or 2 degrees latitude.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what lateral minimum applies to supersonic aircraft operating above FL 275?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-4c1, 60 NM or 1 degree latitude applies between supersonic aircraft operating above FL 275.

choices.text: 23 NM.
60 NM or 1 degree latitude.
50 NM.
90 NM or 1.5 degrees latitude.

items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to turbojet aircraft operating below FL 200 in the San Juan Oceanic CTA/FIR outside the WATRS area?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-3c, 20 minutes applies between turbojet aircraft operating below FL 200 in the San Juan Oceanic (outside the WATRS area), Miami Oceanic, and Houston Oceanic CTAs/FIRs.

choices.text: 10 minutes.
15 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to nonturbojet aircraft operating outside the WATRS in the New York CTA/FIR?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-3d3, 30 minutes applies between nonturbojet aircraft operating outside of the WATRS in the New York CTA/FIR. Inside WATRS the minimum is 20 minutes; outside in NY CTA/FIR it is 30 minutes.

choices.text: 15 minutes.
20 minutes.
10 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what lateral minimum applies between aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval that are operating east of 55 degrees West?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-4f, 120 NM applies between aircraft operating east of 55 degrees West. West of 55W the minimum for non-approved aircraft is 90 NM; east of 55W it increases to 120 NM.

choices.text: 90 NM.
60 NM.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, reciprocal track aircraft without lateral separation must maintain vertical separation for how long before and after the estimated passing time?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-3a4, where lateral separation is not provided, vertical separation must be provided at least 10 minutes before and after the time the aircraft are estimated to pass or are estimated to have passed.

choices.text: 5 minutes before and after estimated passing.
10 minutes before and after estimated passing.
15 minutes before and after estimated passing.
Vertical separation is not required for reciprocal track aircraft in the Pacific.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, when using distance-based minima without ADS-C, what action must be taken if an aircraft fails to report its position within 3 minutes of the expected time?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-3d3, if an aircraft fails to report its position within 3 minutes after the expected time, the controller must take action to establish communication. If communication is not established within 8 minutes, action must be taken to apply another form of separation.

choices.text: Immediately apply another form of separation.
Wait 10 minutes before taking any action.
Take action to establish communication; if not established within 8 minutes, apply another form of separation.
No action is required as distance-based minima remain valid without position reports.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, when DME/RNAV longitudinal separation is applied in the North Pacific high-control areas, what is the maximum duration for which the procedure may continue without direct voice communications?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-3d3, application of DME/RNAV separation without direct voice communications may not continue for more than 90 minutes.

choices.text: 30 minutes.
60 minutes.
120 minutes.
90 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, when DME reports are used to establish longitudinal distance between two turbojet aircraft on the NOPAC route system, what minimum applies?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-3d1, 30 NM applies between aircraft when DME reports or radar observations are used to establish the distance. Otherwise at least 40 NM based on RNAV must be applied.

choices.text: 30 NM with DME; 40 NM with RNAV.
20 NM with DME; 30 NM with RNAV.
40 NM with DME; 50 NM with RNAV.
50 NM regardless of method.

items.situation: An aircraft in oceanic controlled airspace requests a weather deviation to avoid a thunderstorm cell. Another aircraft is on a converging track at the same altitude, 30 NM away. You determine that by climbing the requesting aircraft to FL 370 you can establish vertical separation with the conflicting traffic before they converge.
items.question_text: What is the proper sequence of actions?

choices.text: Issue the deviation clearance immediately; establish separation afterwards.
Deny the deviation because any horizontal conflict automatically means denial.
Ask the pilot to report when clear of weather before taking any separation action.
First establish vertical separation by clearing the aircraft to climb, then issue the deviation clearance once separation is assured.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-10, when there is conflicting traffic in the horizontal dimension, ATC must separate aircraft by establishing vertical separation, then issue clearance to deviate from track. The separation must be established first.
items.para_context: PROCEDURES FOR WEATHER DEVIATIONS AND OTHER CONTINGENCIES IN

items.situation: You are the accepting controller for an oceanic flight. The transferring facility has forwarded the flight data, but the aircraft will arrive at a different flight level than your sector plan requires. The transfer point is still 15 minutes away.
items.question_text: May you issue an amended clearance to change the aircraft's altitude before the transfer point?

choices.text: Yes, as the accepting controller you have full authority to amend the clearance at any time.
No, you must obtain prior approval from the transferring unit before altering the clearance.
Yes, but only if the altitude change is less than 4,000 feet.
No, altitude changes are prohibited until 5 minutes after the transfer point.

items.explanation: Per 8-2-2e, the accepting unit must not alter the clearance of an aircraft that has not yet reached the transfer of control point without the prior approval of the transferring unit.

items.situation: An aircraft at FL 170 is crossing from offshore airspace (using local altimeter setting QNH 29.85) into oceanic airspace (standard setting QNE 29.92). Another aircraft is at FL 180 in oceanic airspace on a converging course. Vertical separation of 1,000 feet appears to exist based on assigned flight levels.
items.question_text: What must the controller ensure before relying on this vertical separation?

choices.text: No action needed; assigned flight levels guarantee separation regardless of altimeter setting.
Instruct both aircraft to use the same altimeter setting after crossing.
Take control action to ensure the QNH-to-QNE difference does not compromise the actual vertical separation.
The transition rule only applies above FL 180, so no action is needed below that altitude.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-1, when vertical separation is applied between aircraft crossing the offshore/oceanic boundary below FL 180, control action must be taken to ensure altimeter setting differences (QNE vs QNH) do not compromise separation.

items.situation: An RNAV-equipped aircraft with a functioning transponder is transitioning from oceanic airspace toward your radar coverage area. You have a radar-identified aircraft at the same altitude on a converging course. Radar separation standards are being maintained between the identified aircraft and all observed targets. However, the transitioning aircraft is a slow-moving turboprop and there is heavy traffic ahead. If radar identification is delayed, you estimate you would need 4 minutes to establish nonradar separation, but with the aircraft's slow speed and traffic conditions, you are not confident you could establish it in time.
items.question_text: May you apply the radar identification transition procedure to this aircraft?

choices.text: Yes, because the aircraft is RNAV equipped and has a standard transponder.
Yes, because radar separation exists between the identified aircraft and all observed targets.
No, because the procedure requires both aircraft to be radar identified before application.
No, the procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time to establish other approved separation if radar identification is delayed.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-5h, the procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time for the controller to establish other approved separation in the event of a delay or inability to establish radar identification, considering factors including aircraft performance, type, speed, weather, traffic, workload, and frequency congestion.

items.situation: A temporary moving airspace reservation is active along your sector. The reservation is moving eastbound at 450 knots. You have an aircraft at FL 350 on a route that would pass behind the reservation. The aircraft can be cleared to maintain longitudinal separation behind the last aircraft operating within the moving reservation.
items.question_text: How should longitudinal separation from the moving reservation be applied?

choices.text: Ensure the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation.
Ensure vertical separation with a buffer of one-half the lateral minimum around the reservation.
Clear the aircraft so its protected airspace does not overlap the reservation area.
Apply the same minimum as for a stationary reservation.

items.explanation: Per 8-6-3b, longitudinal separation from a moving reservation is applied by ensuring the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation.

items.situation: You have cleared an IFR flight to climb in VFR conditions in the Miami CTA at the pilot's request during daylight hours. The aircraft reports that scattered clouds are building and VFR conditions may become impractical at the higher altitude.
items.question_text: What must the controller do?

choices.text: Cancel the VFR climb clearance and return the aircraft to its previously assigned altitude immediately.
Issue alternative instructions because there is a possibility VFR conditions may become impractical.
Do nothing; the pilot is responsible for maintaining VFR conditions.
Issue an IFR clearance to the same altitude.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-5b1, if there is a possibility that VFR conditions may become impractical, the controller must issue alternative instructions. This is a proactive controller obligation, not a pilot-only responsibility.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, what distance verification requirement applies when using 50 NM longitudinal minima without ADS-C?

choices.text: Distance verification is only required at entry and exit points.
Verification is automatic through CPDLC; no controller action is needed.
Distance verification must be obtained from each aircraft at least every 24 minutes.
Distance verification must be obtained every 10 minutes.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-3d2, distance verification must be obtained from each aircraft at least every 24 minutes to verify that separation is maintained.

items.situation: You are applying DME/RNAV longitudinal separation between two turbojet aircraft on the NOPAC route system in the North American ICAO Region. DME reports confirm 35 NM separation. The preceding aircraft is assigned Mach 0.82, and the following aircraft is requesting Mach 0.84.
items.question_text: What must be true of the Mach number assignment before applying this separation?

choices.text: The preceding aircraft must be assigned the same or greater Mach number than the following aircraft.
Both aircraft must be assigned the same Mach number; no speed difference is permitted.
The following aircraft may be faster as long as the 30 NM minimum is maintained.
Mach number is irrelevant to DME/RNAV separation.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-3d4, the preceding aircraft must be assigned the same or greater Mach number than the following aircraft when DME/RNAV separation is applied.

items.front: What three geographic areas permit VFR flight plan operations in Oceanic FIRs?
items.back: 1. Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs below FL 180; 2. Oakland FIR within less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline in controlled airspace; 3. All Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs.

items.front: What are the two mandatory conditions that must both be met before any non-RVSM aircraft may enter RVSM airspace?
items.back: 1. The aircraft must meet the criteria of an excepted aircraft; AND 2. The aircraft must be previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC. Both conditions are required.

items.front: When must control of an aircraft be transferred from one ATC unit to another?
items.back: At the time the aircraft is estimated to cross the control boundary or at such other point or time agreed upon by the two units. Per 8-2-2b.

items.front: What two conditions must both be met for course divergence separation to exist when aircraft enter oceanic airspace?
items.back: 1. Aircraft are established on courses that diverge by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established; AND 2. Aircraft are horizontally radar separated and separation is increasing at the edge of known radar coverage. Per 8-5-2.

items.front: What three methods may be used to separate aircraft from a temporary moving airspace reservation?
items.back: 1. Lateral — protected airspace does not overlap the time-dependent reservation area; 2. Longitudinal — appropriate minimum ahead of first or behind last aircraft in the reservation; 3. Vertical — vertical separation plus buffer of one-half lateral minimum around the time-dependent area. Per 8-6-3.

items.front: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what are the longitudinal minima for nonturbojet aircraft?
items.back: 20 minutes within the West Atlantic Route System (WATRS); 30 minutes outside WATRS. Per 8-7-3c.

items.front: What are the five lateral separation tiers in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: 23 NM (RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180, with CPDLC/ADS-C monitoring); 50 NM (RNP 4 or 10 in NY/San Juan/Miami Atlantic); 60 NM/1° (supersonic above FL275, MNPS/NAT HLA); 90 NM/1.5° (non-RNP in WATRS/San Juan/Miami Atlantic); 120 NM/2° (others not covered above). Per 8-7-4.

items.front: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what longitudinal minima apply to nonturbojet aircraft?
items.back: 20 minutes in WATRS; 20 minutes below FL 200 in Miami, Houston, and San Juan CTAs/FIRs; 30 minutes outside WATRS in the New York CTA/FIR. Per 8-8-3d.

items.front: What are the six lateral separation tiers in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.back: 23 NM (RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180, with CPDLC/ADS-C); 50 NM (RNP 4 or 10 in NY/San Juan/Houston/Miami); 60 NM (supersonic FL275+ in NY, or FL450+ elsewhere, or MNPS/NAT HLA); 90 NM (non-RNP west of 55W); 100 NM (others west of 55W); 120 NM (east of 55W). Per 8-8-4.

items.front: In the Pacific ICAO Region, what are the time-based longitudinal minima before special reductions?
items.back: 15 minutes between aircraft (general); 10 minutes between turbojet aircraft on same or continuously diverging tracks (level, climbing, or descending); or Mach Number Technique minima per 8-3-3. Reciprocal tracks without lateral separation require vertical separation at least 10 minutes before and after estimated passing. Per 8-9-3a.

items.front: What are the three lateral separation tiers in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.back: 50 NM (RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved in designated airspace); 23 NM (RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180 with CPDLC and ADS-C monitoring via ATOP); 100 NM (aircraft not covered by the above). Per 8-9-4.

items.front: What are the DME/RNAV longitudinal separation requirements in the North American ICAO Region NOPAC route system?
items.back: 30 NM with DME reports or radar observations; 40 NM with RNAV otherwise. The preceding aircraft must be assigned same or greater Mach number. Both aircraft must be advised of each other including distance. Without direct voice, may not continue beyond 90 minutes. Per 8-10-3d.

items.front: What are the three lateral separation tiers in the North American ICAO Region?
items.back: 50 NM (RNP 4 or RNP 10 in designated airspace); 23 NM (RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180 with CPDLC/ADS-C monitoring via ATOP — Anchorage Oceanic and Continental CTAs only, not Arctic CTA); 90 NM (aircraft not covered by the above). Per 8-10-4.
